data.title: Simon Property Group: On Its Way To $200
data.description:
It's been almost 2 years since my previous article on Simon Property Group (NYSE:SPG) entitled "Simon Property Group: One Of The Best 7% Dividend Yields". In this article, I will reiterate my belief that SPG is one of the best dividend stocks out there, but the yield is not as appealing as it was back then, since the share price has increased by more than 70%. In fact, SPG has outperformed the main indices over the past five years, as the world has recovered from the brutal coronavirus pandemic.

Interestingly, back then the dividend yield was a compelling 7%, and the per-share dividend has increased substantially, which renders an even higher yield on cost. This has been excellent for those who were brave enough to buy in the early recovery days of the pandemic, when the share price was in the $70s or lower, with a yield on cost now well above 10%. But enough about the past - as always, we need to remain forward-looking.

The reality is that Simon has evolved quite a bit over the past few years, but not through acquiring many new assets, but by redeveloping its existing properties and significantly improving the overall quality of its portfolio. After all, it is all about supply and demand. The supply of high-quality malls remains more or less constant, as very few new malls are being built, while demand is extremely strong. As a result, tenant sales are hovering around all-time highs. Consequently, these malls are becoming even more valuable, with higher occupancy rates and higher rents. This, in turn, has led to a boost in FFO per share, with operating cash flows for Simon also at all-time highs.

Yet, despite all this concrete progress, the share price remains well below the record high of approximately $230 in 2016. It may feel like yesterday, but nearly a decade has passed! Given the company's momentum and best-in-class, irreplaceable properties, I believe that SPG can comfortably surpass the $200 mark and move toward the $250-$300 range.

Another factor is SPG's fortress balance sheet, which enables it to continually redevelop its properties and potentially return to offense by pursuing accretive acquisitions, as it did with Taubman. One standout candidate would be Macerich (MAC), the only high-quality listed mall REIT remaining, which SPG once proposed to acquire for $91 per share. Today, Macerich trades below $20 and it has undergone significant financial and portfolio restructuring. I have written about Macerich in the past - it is a great company with exceptional malls, that is trying to find its way through aggressive deleveraging. In addition, I see attractive opportunities in Europe, starting with Klepierre, in which Simon already owns a 22% stake. Alternatively, Simon could choose to diversify into non-mall assets, given its growing focus on mixed-use properties - an approach I strongly recommend.

One notable change since my last article is that Simon has begun monetizing its stakes in its retail tenants, an area many were sceptical about. Simon has profited significantly from this strategy. Specifically, during Q1 2024, Simon sold its remaining interest in Authentic Brands Group for cash proceeds of $1.2 billion, resulting in a pre-tax gain of $414.8 million. Not bad at all.

That said, and something I encourage, Simon continues to maintain a strategic stake in Catalyst Brands (previously known as SPARC group prior to its merger with JCPenney). Overall, through various vehicles, Simon has invested in several distressed tenants, including, among others, well-known brands such as Aéropostale, Brooks Brothers, Lucky Brand, Forever 21 and Nautica. This model has proven effective and, particularly in times of turmoil, helps support the health of Simon's centers. Currently, Simon remains a strategic shareholder in Catalyst Brands, which generates around $10 billion in revenue, operates 1,800 store locations, employes 60,000 people, and has $1 billion of liquidity. In other words, Simon's stake in Catalyst could eventually be worth billions.

So, what can Simon investors expect going forward? Most importantly, Simon has built an exceptional platform of trophy assets that generate tremendous levels of cash flow. It is fair to say that Simon is a cash machine, standing out not only among mall peers but also compared to most REITs in general. In particular, this will be the first time in Simon's history that operating cash flow is poised to surpass the $4 billion mark.

This substantial cash flow generation comfortably covers the cash dividend, with significant room for grow given that the payout ratio is below 70%. Before the pandemic, in 2019, the dividend was $8.3 per share (the Company's record) and now it appears poised to surpass that level.

In total amounts, cash dividends amount to approximately $2.6 billion on an annualized basis, leaving a surplus of more approximately $1.4 billion each year, based on operating cash flow in excess of $4 billion as discussed above. This surplus can be deployed across a wide range of initiatives, including redevelopments, acquisitions, and debt repayments, as well as other initiatives to reward shareholders such buybacks - an area where Simon has been active, and which is uncommon for most REITs, particularly in terms of meaningfully reducing the share count.

In other words, it is reasonable to assume that SPG's cash machine will continue to boost performance metrics, operating cash flow, and FFO per share, which in turn should support a higher share price. Investor can expect Simon to keep investing in mixed-use developments, including residential complexes, and to increase its focus on urban town centers and master projects such as Brickell City Center in Miami. Performance indicators, such as occupancy (currently above 95%), tenant sales per square foot (currently $736), and base minimum rent per square foot (currently $58.70), are likely to remain remain at or near record levels, given the strong appeal of Simon's malls and town centers. They Company can also be expected to boost guidance, as it usually is very conservative, a practice that it has followed for several years, as it has historically been very conservative in its forecasts.

Simon is also expected to maintain and strengthen its already unusually strong liquidity position, which stood at $9.2 billion as of June 30, 2025, consisting of $1.8 billion of cash on hand (including its share of joint venture cash), and $7.4 billion of available capacity under its revolving credit facilities. With its A-rated balance sheet, Simon has ample ability to tap into the credit markets, as demonstrated by its recent $1.5 Billion senior notes offering, featuring long maturities (7.8 years average term) and an attractive weighted average coupon rate of 4.775%.

Ultimately, and most importantly, investors can expect a solid, sustainable, and very-well covered dividend, currently yielding in excess of 5% and poised to grow over time. I would not be surprised if SPG pursues additional acquisitions, delivered superior organic performance, and drove operating cash flow to surpass the $6 billion mark over the next 5 to 10 years. It goes without saying that, should this happen, the stock is well on its way to $300. This remain a long-term play for income-seeking investors, who will also benefit from capital appreciation over time, in line with the Company's performance. The so-called "retail apocalypse" had many worried over the past decade, but the outlook is now very promising for owners of these irreplaceable malls and town centers, who also have the ability to expand globally.

data.title: Institutional Investors And A Contrarian Look At CNX Resources
data.description:
Investment thesis

CNX Resources Corporation (NYSE:CNX) has had problems recently, with negative earnings in several quarters until turning around in the latest earnings report, Q2-2025. That's led to a loss of investor confidence and falling prices.

Three of four Seeking Alpha analyses have provided Sell ratings since December 1, 2024. Wall Street analysts are equally bearish with one Strong Buy rating, as opposed to seven Holds, three Sells, and three Strong Sells.

In this article, we’ll arrive at a contrary conclusion, using some of the corporate characteristics valued by institutional investors.

About CNX

It is an independent natural gas and midstream company that acquires property, explores, develops, and produces natural gas in the Appalachian Basin. CNX operates through two segments, Shale and Coalbed Methane, or CBM. In 2024, 93% of its revenue came from shale natural gas and 7% came from coalbed methane.

The midstream assets include 2,700 miles of natural gas gathering pipelines as well as natural gas processing facilities. The company was founded in 1860 and is headquartered in Canonsburg, PA.

As I reported in a May 15, 2025 article, the firm’s key metric is FCF or Free Cash Flow. In its second quarter earnings summary, it reported that it had delivered its 22nd consecutive quarter of free cash flow.

About institutional investors

Institutional investors, the so-called “smart money”, own almost all outstanding stock in CNX. Seeking Alpha puts the proportion at 91.94% or 3.91 billion out of 4.25 billion shares.

Nasdaq puts ownership at 99.87%, a difference that may be due to structural issues or derivatives. It also reports that 147 institutional investors increased their positions in the most recent quarter, by 8.1 million shares. Another 207 holders decreased their positions by 14.3 million shares, and 63 holders, representing 118 million shares made no changes.

Even using the Seeking Alpha figure of 91.94% means most price changes for CNX are driven by these investors, who tend to move large blocks of shares at a time.

Are they the ones, then, that pulled the price down last November? It’s quite possible, after all these institutions tend to have teams rather than individuals doing their due diligence. And they would have researched tariffs and other threats that were emerging late last year.

CNX isn’t the only Appalachian producer to enjoy a lot of institutional support. These other Appalachian gas producers: EQT Corporation (EQT), Range Resources Corporation (RRC), and Expand Energy Corporation (EXE) also do well:

  • EQT: 90.10%
  • RRC: 98.87%
  • EXE: 92.07%

It seems that fund managers like not just CNX, but this part of the energy industry.

Risks and rewards

As fiduciaries, institutional investors have a duty to their clients to provide a steady stream of income without taking undue risks. For example, pension fund managers may have millions of current and future pensioners who depend on fund income and/or interest.

Let’s start by assessing its risk profile, starting with its 24-month Beta. It clocks in at 0.66, well below the general market’s Beta of 1.00. That means it is less volatile than the market as a whole; in other words, if the market moves up or down 1%, the stock will move 0.66% in the same direction.

Low Beta stocks are considered defensive stocks; many utilities have lower volatility than the market, and so we might consider CNX a utility or something comparable. That’s the sort of risk exposure many institutional investors like to see.

Fellow Appalachian producers EQT, Range, and Expand have similar profiles, at 0.87, 0.74, and 0.55 respectively.

Looking at risks more specifically, CNX might suffer financially if gas prices fall, if new technologies quickly replace natural gas, or if regulatory conditions become onerous. But in any market-wide tumble, these producers will take lesser hits.

On the reward side, the following three-year chart shows that returns on equity have gone down in recent years.

CNX began slipping slightly later but fell nevertheless, as did the other three. But if you want a reason to stick with these stocks, take a look at their previous high ROEs:

  • Expand: 90.5%, Q1-2023
  • Range: 86.5%, Q1-2023
  • EQT: 44.35%, Q1-2023
  • CNX: 80.26%, Q3-2023.

There is no guarantee they will ever again reach those highs, but we know they have done it in the past, so we know it is possible in the future.

And how do these past and current returns compare with the market? If we turn to price returns, we see CNX has outperformed the S&P 500 through most of the past five years:

We saw that CNX has a lower risk position than the market as a whole; it has a lower Beta and its risk profile is similar to that of utilities. However, it can deliver greater capital gains than most utilities or bonds. If I were a fund manager, I might not be in a rush to sell my CNX stock.

Sustained value

First, CNX is a low-cost producer, which contributes to longevity. Low-cost producers can keep producing for longer when the costs of exploration and production increase. In addition, it can remain profitable longer when prices fall, compared to higher-cost producers.

This strategy or tactic appears to be part of the firm’s DNA, so it likely has taken hold all the way from the wellhead to the executive offices.

Second, at the end of 2024, it had proved natural gas reserves of 8.54 trillion cubic feet equivalent according to its second quarter earnings summary. During that year it produced 550.8 net billion cubic feet equivalent. So 2024 production represented roughly 6.45% of its natural gas reserves.

That suggests CNX has enough reserves to last 15.50 years, however, reality is more complex. While continuing to produce, the company will search for new reserves through its own exploration and development efforts. It may also buy reserves; last December it purchased Apex Energy II, which brought it 36,000 net acres in Pennsylvania, 8,600 acres of underdeveloped Utica Shale, and 12,600 acres of undeveloped Marcellus Shale.

Third, in addition to its production operations, it has an extensive network of pipelines. It should generate significant revenues regardless of what happens on the production side.

On the risks side, if renewables progress more quickly than expected there is a possibility natural gas prices could fall, and if they do, then there may be less demand for pipeline space. In that case, both arms of the business would suffer financially.

Generally speaking, though, the data and information suggests CNX is well positioned to continue delivering sustained value.

Stability

First, a look at financial stability, using this 10-year chart of revenue, net income, and EPS:

Net income and basic EPS took a hit in the wake of COVID-19 pandemic, but then recovered and more as that situation eased and economic conditions returned to normal. Since then, they declined or perhaps more accurately we might say they reverted to the mean. Finally, in Q2-2025 they began to rise again.

Revenue has been on a path of its own, staying relatively flat through the pandemic, picking up post-pandemic, and then it too reverted to the mean.

Overall, this aspect of the business has been relatively stable, or at least as stable as it might be when macroeconomic circumstances change dramatically.

Second, have management and the strategy been consistent in recent years?

CEO and director Nicholas J. DeIuliis has been with CNX for more than 30 years, and its leader since 2014. CFO and President Alan K. Shepard rejoined the company in 2020 and has held the top financial position since 2022. It appears CNX can retain its senior officers, providing both continuity and stability.

Third, the company has operated in one form or another for 160 years, a good sign. In 2017, it changed its name from CONSOL Energy and began to focus on natural gas exploration and production, with an emphasis on the Appalachian basin.

Its strategy has also evolved to focus on low-cost, high-return assets, rather than production at any cost. And, its strategy has evolved to focus on free cash flow rather than earnings.

There are risks to each aspect of its strategy. As we saw above, macroeconomic conditions can affect demand for natural gas and CNX’s financials. There is always a possibility that the apparent calm in executive offices may be disrupted, and management may make future strategic decisions that hurt rather than help its operating income.

Again, though, there appear to be no immediate threats, and it is quite likely CNX will continue to be a stable company with relatively stable net income and earnings.

Price targets and ratings

For context, this is a 10-year price chart for CNX, a chart that shows a modestly volatile, but steady upward trend:

Ironically, the rising trend began in the first quarter of 2020, as COVID-19 became established. Second, the price dropped sharply in early July, but I expect it will revert to the trend in the near future, given the positives it offers investors.

This is CNX’s estimated intrinsic value, using the calculator at OmniCalculator.com:

The inputs were basic EPS TTM, an estimated earnings growth rate based on the average of Wall Street analysts’ forecasts for 2025, 2026, and 2027, the current corporate bond yield, and the closing price on August 26, 2025.

Based on intrinsic value of $70.69, a 58.7% margin of safety, and the confidence of the institutional investors, I am upgrading my previous Buy to a Strong Buy. There has been one other Seeking Alpha analyst rating in the past 90 days, a Sell.

The Wall Street analysts have an average price target of $31.69, an increase of 8.56%. Collectively, they have a Hold rating, with one Strong Buy, seven Holds, three Sells, and three Strong Sells. The Quant system has a Hold rating.

Conclusion

I respect institutional investors, most of which aim to preserve capital and generate income without undue risk. Their overwhelming position in CNX Resources means they continue to have a solid interest.

And that interest has been earned by CNX’s positive risk/reward profile, strong reserves, low-cost strategy, and financial and management stability.

data.title: TD Bank Q3 Earnings Analysis: Positive Progress Continues
data.description:
Toronto-Dominion Bank (NYSE:TD)(TSX:TD:CA) joined most of its Canadian banking peers and reported better than expected earnings this week. Results were encouraging because the company is showing investors it can grow and execute while in the penalty box with U.S. regulators.

(All figures are in CAD unless indicated otherwise)

Introduction

There's been only one issue that has dominated TD Bank shares over the last couple of years. It isn't the state of the Canadian housing market, nor the financial health of borrowers, or even economic instability as Canada and the U.S. continue to squabble over tariffs.

No, investors are obsessed with TD's legal troubles and how they will impact the stock going forward.

A very quick background for those who aren't familiar. TD was fined more than US$3B in 2024 after U.S. regulators started an investigation into the company's anti-money laundering practices. Regulators also put an asset cap in place, ordering the bank's U.S. subsidiaries to not exceed US$434B in assets.

Wells Fargo was also slapped with an asset cap when it ran afoul with regulators, and shares struggled for years amid that cap. Investors feared TD would suffer the same fate.

Bulls pointed out the differences between TD and Wells Fargo, saying that TD could just shift excess capital back to Canada, use it to repurchase shares, or set it aside for an acquisition outside of the United States.

The bulls are winning the argument -- at least so far. TD shares are up more than 30% in the last year and are more than 40% higher than their 52-week low, which was set in December. It's been an excellent run.

The run looks poised to continue after the company released better than expected Q3 earnings. Let's dive into those earnings to see what exactly happened.

Toronto-Dominion Bank Q3 earnings overview

TD reported total revenue of $15.3B in its most recent quarter, which ended on July 31st. That translates into an 8% increase compared to the same quarter last year. This beat consensus analyst expectations by $1.59B, or more than 10%.

The company also beat on the bottom line. Adjusted earnings came in at $2.20 per share, 7.3% higher than estimates. Analysts expected the company to earn $2.05 per share in the quarter. This was 7% higher than last year's result and 12% higher than the previous quarter.

TD lost $0.14 per share in the same quarter last year, but that was because it took a one-time charge and set aside the money to pay what it knew were upcoming fines.

The company reported a return on equity of 11.3% for the quarter, or 13.2% on an adjusted basis. That number was higher if we look at the return on tangible equity, which was 13.6% on a reported basis and 15.8% on an adjusted basis.

TD also reported lower provisions for credit losses. That number totaled $971M for the quarter, which was a $370M decline compared to the quarter before and a $101M decline versus the same quarter last year.

This graph does an excellent job illustrating the trend in credit losses. They were marching steadily higher over the last year before falling in the most recent quarter. That's an encouraging sign.

Like its peers, TD is known for its history of paying a steadily growing dividend over time. Canadian dividend investors are usually curious whether the banks increased their dividend whenever quarterly earnings come out. TD did not increase its payout this time around but should hike its dividend at the end of November when it reports Q4 earnings.

Let's take a closer look at each part of the company, starting with TD's Canadian Banking operations.

Canadian Banking

Overall, TD's Canadian results were good. The company achieved record revenue, earnings, deposits, and loan volumes. Loan volumes were up 4%, and deposit volumes were up a similar amount.

Net interest margins increased marginally to 2.83%.

Revenue came in at $5.21B, which was a 5% increase over last year. Expenses increased by about 5% as well, surpassing $2.06B. That works out to net income of $1.95B, which was 4% higher than it was last year.

We'll note that TD's provision for credit losses (PCLs) from its Canadian division was up slightly on a year-over-year basis, increasing $28M to $463M. But those same PCLs were down significantly versus the last quarter, falling $159M. That's a good trend, especially amid an uncertain macro environment for Canada as a whole.

TD delivered a stellar 32.5% return on equity for its Canadian operations, which is actually slightly lower than the 34.1% number it posted in the same quarter last year.

Overall, results from TD's Canadian operations were solid, with PCLs coming in at a very reasonable level. But some of the other parts of the company did even better.

U.S. retail banking

All eyes were on TD's U.S. retail results as investors worry that the regulator-enforced asset cap will weigh down this part of the company over the long term.

Despite the tough conditions, TD did pretty well in the U.S. in the third quarter. On an adjusted basis it reported a 5% increase in revenue, with the top line increasing to US$2.72B. Expenses were 13% higher, increasing to US$1.73B, although that's to be expected. TD is investing in staff training and increased compliance to ensure it stays on the good side of regulators going forward.

Net income on an adjusted basis -- excluding its stake in Charles Schwab, which was sold earlier in 2025 -- was US$695M or $956M once converted back to Canadian Dollars. That's a 4% decline on a constant currency basis, or 3% when expressed in the company's reporting currency.

TD has also been restructuring its balance sheet in the U.S. to stay under the asset cap but also give it some opportunity to grow. When the cap was first set, TD was pretty much at the limit. But it has let loans run-off, made some non-core asset sales, and simplified the overall business. It now has US$386B in assets in the United States, US$48B under the asset cap.

This room should allow the company to start growing the U.S. part of its business again, which is positive for future growth while still under the limitations set by regulators. Remember, we really don't have any idea how long TD will be under asset limits. It could be years.

Wealth management and insurance

TD delivered good results from its wealth management and insurance division.

Revenue was up 10%, buoyed by higher insurance premiums. Wealth management fees were also higher in the quarter as strong stock markets drove up assets under management. AUM increased by 9% on a year-over-year basis.

Insurance expenses were 6% lower than the previous year. 2024 saw an increase in severe weather events that did not repeat in 2025. Excluding these insurance expenses, overall expenses did creep 5% higher.

Net income for both businesses under this umbrella increased to $703M. That was 63% higher than last year, although poor insurance results weighed heavily on Q3 2024 results. Wealth management profits increased 26% compared to last year.

I'd also like to point out the strong returns on equity TD's insurance and wealth management businesses generate. Wealth management return on equity was 62.4% and insurance return on equity was 24.7%. Overall, this part of the company had a 44.7% return on equity for the quarter, which was significantly higher than last year's number -- and higher than the rest of the bank. An excellent result.

Bullish Canadian investors are using TD's self-service broker more, too. Average trades per day on the TD Direct Investing app are up nearly 20% versus the same quarter last year. And the trend is heading in the right direction. Investors are increasingly short-term oriented these days, which will benefit TD's self-directed platform.

Wholesale Banking

Finally, let's talk about Wholesale Banking. This is the most volatile part of TD's business since it's largely tied to mergers, acquisitions, IPOs, and other capital markets events. If the economy is lackluster or there just isn't much happening, results will suffer.

Fortunately, results were strong. Revenue was up 15% versus Q3 2024, increasing to just over $2.06B. Global markets led the way with a 23% increase, while investment banking revenues crept 4% higher.

Unfortunately, expenses increased right alongside revenues. They were 14% higher, jumping to just under $1.5B. Net income was $398M for the quarter, up 26% higher than last year.

One reason why profits jumped so much was that provisions for credit losses plunged. That number was $71M in the quarter, a $47M decrease compared to last year. That's nearly a 40% drop.

From a return on equity standpoint, this is one of the weaker parts of TD's overall business. Wholesale Banking's adjusted ROE was just 9.9% in the quarter, but that was an improvement compared to last year's number, 9.4%.

Risks

The market's reaction to TD's earnings was a little bit peculiar. Shares fell nearly 4% in early trading on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

TD shares have rallied significantly in the last year and these numbers are pretty good. I believe the negative reaction is being caused by investors taking their short-term profits. The risk is that investors continue to do that and drive shares lower in the near-term.

Another risk is TD's turnaround plan. The company has been telling investors for months that it will unveil the company's direction under new CEO Raymond Chun. That's coming on September 29th, and there's always risk the market will be underwhelmed with the plan and push shares lower.

Finally, I'll point out that TD's valuation is a little stretched. Shares trade at 12.6x forward earnings, which is close to the stock's highest valuation in years. I tend to get a little nervous buying at multi-year valuation highs.

The bottom line

Overall I believe TD's Q3 earnings were solid, and the company took steps in the right direction. The Canadian part of the business grew amid a tough macro environment. Continued progress has been achieved in the U.S. Wealth Management was buoyed by strong markets, and Insurance benefitted from better weather. Even Wholesale Banking, which is notoriously volatile, posted nice results.

Investors should be encouraged, and I wouldn't worry about shares trading lower. I believe this is an overreaction.

Although TD Bank shares trade at a relatively high valuation, I continue to rate them a buy based on continued expected growth from the Canadian business and what I believe will be strong direction from the new CEO.
